items.title: Three ways to launch a Python CLI
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  U(["user shell"])
  U --> A["mycli ..."]
  U --> B["python -m mypkg.cli ..."]
  U --> C["python path/to/cli.py ..."]
  A --> Wrap["installed wrapper\n(generated by pip from [project.scripts])"]
  B --> Mod["python -m loads mypkg/cli/__main__.py\nor module's __main__ block"]
  C --> Direct["script run as __main__\n(path-dependent, fragile)"]
  Wrap --> Main["mypkg.cli:main()"]
  Mod --> Main
  Direct --> Main
items.caption: All paths land in the same `main()` — installed console scripts and `python -m` are the durable mechanisms; ad-hoc script paths are best avoided.

items.title: Thin CLI vs thick CLI
items.mermaid:
flowchart TB
  subgraph Thin["Thin CLI (good)"]
    T1["cli.py\nparse args → call library → format output"] --> T2["mypkg.core (importable, testable)"]
  end
  subgraph Thick["Thick CLI (avoid)"]
    K1["cli.py\nparse args + open files + DB calls\n+ algorithm + print"]
  end
items.caption: Keep parsing/IO separate from business logic. Thin CLIs let you test, reuse, and replace the interface independently of the work.
